title: Try an Example for the dateFacet Type

sections.heading: Step 1: Create a  configuration file and specify the  type.
Step 2: Connect to the Atlas CLI
sections.body:
In your terminal, connect to your cluster from the
Atlas CLI. To learn more, see
[Connect from the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli).

sections.heading: Step 3: Create a  index.
sections.body:
Run the [atlas clusters search indexes create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-clusters-search-indexes-create)
command in your terminal, replacing `<clusterName>` with the
name of the cluster, and `<path-to-file>` with the
path to the `search-index.json` file:```shell
atlas clusters search indexes create --clusterName <cluster-name> --file <path-to-file>
```The MongoDB Search `number` type has the following parameters:| UI Field Name  | JSON Option    | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Default |
| -------------- | -------------- | ------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| Data Type      | type           | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be number.                                                                                                                                                                                            |         |
| Representation | representation | string  | Optional  | Data type of the field to index. Values are:int64 - for indexing large integers without loss of precision and for rounding double values to integers. You can't use this type to index large double values.double - for indexing large double values without rounding. | double  |
| Index Integers | indexIntegers  | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing int32 and int64 type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexDoubles must be true.                                                                                                               | true    |
| Index Doubles  | indexDoubles   | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing double type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexIntegers must be true.                                                                                                                       | true    |The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.Representation ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the [sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes the
`account_id` field with 64-bit integer values. The
following example also:* Indexes all other integer values in the `account_id` field.

* Rounds any decimal values and indexes small double type
  values in the `account_id` field.```json
{
  "name": "default",
  "collectionName": "accounts",
  "database": "sample_analytics",
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": false,
    "fields": {
      "account_id": {
        "type": "number",
        "representation": "int64"
      }
    }
  }
}
```Index Integers ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes integer
values and omits doubles values in the the `account_id`.```json
{
  "name": "default",
  "collectionName": "accounts",
  "database": "sample_analytics",
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": false,
    "fields": {
      "account_id": {
        "type": "number",
        "representation": "int64",
        "indexDoubles": false
      }
    }
  }
}
```Index Doubles ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes
`double` type values and omits the 32-bit and 64-bit integer
values in the `bathrooms` field.```json
{
  "name": "default",
  "collectionName": "listingsAndReviews",
  "database": "sample_airbnb",
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": false,
    "fields": {
      "bathrooms": {
        "type": "number",
        "indexIntegers": false
      }
    }
  }
}
```You can use the MongoDB Search `number` type to index fields with numeric
values of `int32`, `int64`, and `double` data types.
You can use the [equals](equals-ref), [range](range-ref), and [near](near-ref)
operators to query indexed fields of type `number`. You can also run a
[faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) query on `number` type fields.> **Note:** To query numeric values in arrays, you can use only the [range](range-ref)
> operator.MongoDB Search automatically indexes all numeric fields in indexes created after
[July 2023](fts20230710) for [sorting](sort-ref) the MongoDB Search
results. For preexisting indexes, you must rebuild the index to use
number fields in the index for sorting. To learn more, see
[index update](fts-index-rebuild) and [sorting](sort-ref).If you enable [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings), MongoDB Search
automatically indexes fields of type dateFacet. You can
use the **Visual Editor** or the  **JSON Editor** in the Atlas UI to
index fields as the dateFacet type.

sections.heading: Step 1: Connect to your cluster using .
sections.body:
Open [Compass](https://www.mongodb.com/try/download/compass) and connect to your cluster. For
detailed instructions, see [Compass](atlas-connect-via-compass).

sections.heading: Step 2: Specify the database and collection.
sections.body: On the Database screen, click the name of the database, then click the name of the collection.

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the  index.
Step 1: Connect to the deployment by using .
sections.body:
In your terminal, connect to your Atlas cloud-hosted
deployment or local deployment from [mongosh](mongodb-shell). For detailed
instructions on how to connect, see
[Connect to a Deployment](mongodb-shell/connect).

sections.heading: Step 2: Switch to the database that contains the collection for which you want to create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**

```shell
 use <database>
```

**Output:**

```shell
switched to db <database>
```

sections.heading: Step 3: Run the  method to create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**```shell
db.<collection>.createSearchIndex(
  "default",
    {
      "mappings": {
        "dynamic": true|false,
        "fields": {
        "<field-name>": {
            "type": "number",
            "representation": "int64|double",
            "indexIntegers": true|false,
            "indexDoubles": true|false
        }
      }
    }
  }
)
```**Output:**```
default
```The MongoDB Search `number` type has the following parameters:| UI Field Name  | JSON Option    | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Default |
| -------------- | -------------- | ------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| Data Type      | type           | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be number.                                                                                                                                                                                            |         |
| Representation | representation | string  | Optional  | Data type of the field to index. Values are:int64 - for indexing large integers without loss of precision and for rounding double values to integers. You can't use this type to index large double values.double - for indexing large double values without rounding. | double  |
| Index Integers | indexIntegers  | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing int32 and int64 type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexDoubles must be true.                                                                                                               | true    |
| Index Doubles  | indexDoubles   | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing double type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexIntegers must be true.                                                                                                                       | true    |The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.Representation ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the [sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes the
`account_id` field with 64-bit integer values. The
following example also:* Indexes all other integer values in the `account_id` field.

* Rounds any decimal values and indexes small double type
  values in the `account_id` field.**Input:**```shell
use sample_analytics
db.accounts.createSearchIndex(
   "default",
   {
     "mappings": {
       "dynamic": false,
       "fields": {
         "account_id": {
           "type": "number",
           "representation": "int64"
         }
       }
     }
   }
)
```**Output:**```
default
```Index Integers ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes integer
values and omits doubles values in the the `account_id`.**Input:**```shell
use sample_analytics
db.accounts.createSearchIndex(
   "default",
   {
     "mappings": {
       "dynamic": false,
       "fields": {
         "account_id": {
           "type": "number",
           "representation": "int64",
           "indexDoubles": false
         }
       }
     }
   }
)
```**Output:**```
default
```Index Doubles ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes
`double` type values and omits the 32-bit and 64-bit integer
values in the `bathrooms` field.**Input:**```shell
use sample_airbnb
db.listingsAndReviews.createSearchIndex(
   "default",
   {
     "mappings": {
       "dynamic": false,
       "fields": {
         "bathrooms": {
           "type": "number",
           "indexIntegers": false
         }
       }
     }
   }
)
```**Output:**```
default
```You can use the MongoDB Search `number` type to index fields with numeric
values of `int32`, `int64`, and `double` data types.
You can use the [equals](equals-ref), [range](range-ref), and [near](near-ref)
operators to query indexed fields of type `number`. You can also run a
[faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) query on `number` type fields.> **Note:** To query numeric values in arrays, you can use only the [range](range-ref)
> operator.MongoDB Search automatically indexes all numeric fields in indexes created after
[July 2023](fts20230710) for [sorting](sort-ref) the MongoDB Search
results. For preexisting indexes, you must rebuild the index to use
number fields in the index for sorting. To learn more, see
[index update](fts-index-rebuild) and [sorting](sort-ref).If you enable [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings), MongoDB Search
automatically indexes fields of type dateFacet. You can
use the **Visual Editor** or the  **JSON Editor** in the Atlas UI to
index fields as the dateFacet type.Choose your preferred configuration method in the Atlas UI and then select the database and collection.Visual Editor* Click Refine Your Index to configure your index.

* In the Field Mappings section, click Add
  Field Mapping to open the Add Field Mapping window.

* Click Customized Configuration.

* Select the field to index from the Field Name dropdown.

  > **Note:** You can't index fields that contain the dollar (`$`) sign at the
  > start of the field name.

* Click the Data Type dropdown and select
  UUID.

* Configure the field properties for the dateFacet type. To
  learn more, see Field Properties.

* Click Add.JSON EditorThe following is the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) syntax for the dateFacet type.
Replace the default index definition with the following. To learn more
about the fields, see Field Properties.```json
{
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": true|false,
    "fields": {
      "<field-name>": {
        "type": "number",
        "representation": "int64|double",
        "indexIntegers": true|false,
        "indexDoubles": true|false
      }
    }
  }
}
```The MongoDB Search `number` type has the following parameters:| UI Field Name  | JSON Option    | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Default |
| -------------- | -------------- | ------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| Data Type      | type           | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be number.                                                                                                                                                                                            |         |
| Representation | representation | string  | Optional  | Data type of the field to index. Values are:int64 - for indexing large integers without loss of precision and for rounding double values to integers. You can't use this type to index large double values.double - for indexing large double values without rounding. | double  |
| Index Integers | indexIntegers  | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing int32 and int64 type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexDoubles must be true.                                                                                                               | true    |
| Index Doubles  | indexDoubles   | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing double type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexIntegers must be true.                                                                                                                       | true    |The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use the
Visual Editor or JSON (Javascript Object Notation) Editor to configure
these indexes. After you select your preferred configuration method,
select the database and collection, and refine your index to add field
mappings.Representation ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the [sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes the
`account_id` field with 64-bit integer values. The
following example also:- Indexes all other integer values in the `account_id` field.

- Rounds any decimal values and indexes small double type
  values in the `account_id` field.* In the Add Field Mapping window, select
  account\_id from the Field
  Name dropdown.

* Click the Data Type dropdown and select
  Number.

* Modify the Number Properties to set the
  following:

  - Representation to `int64`.

  - Index Integers to `true`.

  - Index Doubles to `true`.

* Click Add.Replace the default index definition with the following
index definition.```json
{
   "mappings": {
      "dynamic": false,
      "fields": {
        "account_id": {
          "type": "number",
          "representation": "int64"
        }
      }
   }
}
```Index Integers ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes integer
values and omits doubles values in the the `account_id`.* In the Add Field Mapping window, select
  account\_id from the Field
  Name dropdown.

* Click the Data Type dropdown and select
  Number.

* Modify the Number Properties to set the
  following:

  - Representation to `int64`.

  - Index Integers to `true`.

  - Index Doubles to `false`.

* Click Add.Replace the default index definition with the following
index definition.```json
{
   "mappings": {
      "dynamic": false,
      "fields": {
        "account_id": {
          "type": "number",
          "representation": "int64",
          "indexDoubles": false
        }
      }
   }
}
```Index Doubles ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes
`double` type values and omits the 32-bit and 64-bit integer
values in the `bathrooms` field.* In the Add Field Mapping window, select
  bathrooms from the Field
  Name dropdown.

* Click the Data Type dropdown and select
  Number.

* Modify the Number Properties to set the
  following:

  - Representation to `doubles`.

  - Index Integers to `false`.

  - Index Doubles to `true`.

* Click Add.Replace the default index definition with the following
index definition.```json
{
   "mappings": {
      "dynamic": false,
      "fields": {
        "bathrooms": {
          "type": "number",
          "indexIntegers": false
        }
      }
   }
}
```You can use the MongoDB Search `number` type to index fields with numeric
values of `int32`, `int64`, and `double` data types.
You can use the [equals](equals-ref), [range](range-ref), and [near](near-ref)
operators to query indexed fields of type `number`. You can also run a
[faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) query on `number` type fields.> **Note:** To query numeric values in arrays, you can use only the [range](range-ref)
> operator.MongoDB Search automatically indexes all numeric fields in indexes created after
[July 2023](fts20230710) for [sorting](sort-ref) the MongoDB Search
results. For preexisting indexes, you must rebuild the index to use
number fields in the index for sorting. To learn more, see
[index update](fts-index-rebuild) and [sorting](sort-ref).If you enable [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings), MongoDB Search
automatically indexes fields of type dateFacet. You can
use the **Visual Editor** or the  **JSON Editor** in the Atlas UI to
index fields as the dateFacet type.

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up the C project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` for this project:

```shell
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
```

Add the C driver to your project by following the instructions in the
[MongoDB C Driver documentation](languages/c/c-driver/current/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Set up a CMake application
sections.body:
To configure your application, create a `CMakeLists.txt` file in
your project directory. Then, add the following code to the file:

```txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

```

The preceding code performs the following actions:

- Configures a C project.

- Creates a `index.out` executable for your application.

- Finds and requires the C driver. Replace the `<version>`
  placeholder with your C driver version number, such as `2.0.0`.

- Links the program to the `libmongoc` library.

> **Note:** In the sample `CMakeLists.txt` file, the `mongoc::mongoc` target
> points to either the static library or the shared library.
> The library type depends on which one is available and
> whichever type the user specifies in the `MONGOC_DEFAULT_IMPORTED_LIBRARY_TYPE`
> CMake configuration setting. If you don't set this value and
> both library types are available, `mongoc::mongoc` uses
> the static library.
>
> You can use the `mongoc::static` target to explicitly use the
> static library or the `mongoc::shared` target to use the shared
> library.

codeBlocks.content:
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

sections.heading: Step 4: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up and initialize the .NET/C# project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` and initialize your project in that directory:

```shell
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
dotnet new console

# Add the MongoDB .NET/C# Driver to your project
dotnet add package MongoDB.Driver
```

For more detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB C# Driver documentation](csharp/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
dotnet new console

# Add the MongoDB .NET/C# Driver to your project
dotnet add package MongoDB.Driver

Step 1: Set up the C++ project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` for this project:

```shell
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
```

For more detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB C++ Driver documentation](languages/cpp/cpp-driver/current/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up and initialize the Go module.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` and initialize your project in that directory:

```shell
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
go mod init atlas-search-project

# Add the MongoDB Go Driver to your project
go get go.mongodb.org/mongo-driver/v2/mongo
```

For more detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB Go Driver documentation](go/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
go mod init atlas-search-project

# Add the MongoDB Go Driver to your project
go get go.mongodb.org/mongo-driver/v2/mongo

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up your Java project with the MongoDB Java driver.
sections.body:
In your IDE, create a new [Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) or
[Gradle](https://gradle.org/) project. Add the Bill of
Materials (BOM) for MongoDB JVM artifacts to your project to
organize dependency versions. The BOM simplifies dependency
management by ensuring that you maintain consistent and compatible
versions of dependencies, such as between the Java driver and
the core driver library. Use the BOM to avoid version conflicts
and simplify upgrades.Select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs
to view instructions for adding the BOM for each dependency manager:MavenAdd the following code to the `dependencyManagement` list in your
`pom.xml` file:```xml
<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>
            <artifactId>mongodb-driver-bom</artifactId>
            <version>5.5.1</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>
```GradleAdd the following code to dependencies list in your
`build.gradle` file:```groovy
dependencies {
    implementation(platform("org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-bom:5.5.1"))
}
```To view a list of dependencies that the BOM manages, see
the [mongodb-driver-bom dependency listing](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/org.mongodb/mongodb-driver-bom/5.51)
on the Maven Repository website.After adding the BOM, select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs to view
instructions for adding the MongoDB Java Sync Driver as a dependency
in your project:MavenAdd the following code to the `dependencies` array in your Maven
project's `pom.xml` file:```xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>    
      <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>    
      <artifactId>mongodb-driver-sync</artifactId>    
      <version>4.11.1</version>    
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

```GradleAdd the following to the `dependencies` array in your Gradle
project's `build.gradle` file:```json
dependencies {
   // MongoDB Java Sync Driver v4.11.0 or later
   implementation 'org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync'
}

```Run your package manager to install the dependencies to your project.For more detailed installation instructions and version compatibility, see
the [MongoDB Java Driver documentation](java/sync/current/get-started).

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Compile and run the file to create the index.
Step 1: Initialize your Node.js project.
sections.body:
```shell
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
npm init -y

# Add the MongoDB Node.js Driver to your project
npm install mongodb
```

For detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB Node Driver documentation](node).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
npm init -y

# Add the MongoDB Node.js Driver to your project
npm install mongodb

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up the Python project.
sections.body:
```shell
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

# Install PyMongo
pip install pymongo
```

For detailed installation instructions, see
[MongoDB Python Driver documentation](languages/python/pymongo-driver/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

# Install PyMongo
pip install pymongo

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
Create a `create_index.py` file in your project directory,
and copy and paste the following code into the file.```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["<database>"]
collection = database["<collection>"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": True|False,
            "fields": {
                "<field_name>":{
                   "type": "number",
                   "representation": "int64|double",
                   "indexIntegers": True|False,
                   "indexDoubles": True|False
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")

```Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.Atlas ClusterYour connection string should use the following format:```
mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
```To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).Local or Self-ManagedYour connection string should use the
following format:```
mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
```To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**```shell
python create_index.py
```**Output:**```
New index name: default
```The MongoDB Search `number` type has the following parameters:| UI Field Name  | JSON Option    | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Default |
| -------------- | -------------- | ------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| Data Type      | type           | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be number.                                                                                                                                                                                            |         |
| Representation | representation | string  | Optional  | Data type of the field to index. Values are:int64 - for indexing large integers without loss of precision and for rounding double values to integers. You can't use this type to index large double values.double - for indexing large double values without rounding. | double  |
| Index Integers | indexIntegers  | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing int32 and int64 type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexDoubles must be true.                                                                                                               | true    |
| Index Doubles  | indexDoubles   | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing double type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexIntegers must be true.                                                                                                                       | true    |The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.Representation ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the [sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes the
`account_id` field with 64-bit integer values. The
following example also:* Indexes all other integer values in the `account_id` field.

* Rounds any decimal values and indexes small double type
  values in the `account_id` field.```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_analytics"]
collection = database["accounts"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": False,
            "fields": {
                "account_id":{
                   "type": "number",
                   "representation": "int64"
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```Index Integers ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes integer
values and omits doubles values in the the `account_id`.```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_analytics"]
collection = database["accounts"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": False,
            "fields": {
                "account_id":{
                   "type": "number",
                   "representation": "int64",
                   "indexDoubles": False
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```Index Doubles ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes
`double` type values and omits the 32-bit and 64-bit integer
values in the `bathrooms` field.```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_airbnb"]
collection = database["listingsAndReviews"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": False,
            "fields": {
                "bathrooms":{
                   "type": "number",
                   "indexIntegers": False
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```You can use the MongoDB Search `number` type to index fields with numeric
values of `int32`, `int64`, and `double` data types.
You can use the [equals](equals-ref), [range](range-ref), and [near](near-ref)
operators to query indexed fields of type `number`. You can also run a
[faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) query on `number` type fields.> **Note:** To query numeric values in arrays, you can use only the [range](range-ref)
> operator.MongoDB Search automatically indexes all numeric fields in indexes created after
[July 2023](fts20230710) for [sorting](sort-ref) the MongoDB Search
results. For preexisting indexes, you must rebuild the index to use
number fields in the index for sorting. To learn more, see
[index update](fts-index-rebuild) and [sorting](sort-ref).If you enable [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings), MongoDB Search
automatically indexes fields of type dateFacet. You can
use the **Visual Editor** or the  **JSON Editor** in the Atlas UI to
index fields as the dateFacet type.

sections.heading: Step 1: Create a  configuration file and specify the  type.
Step 2: Connect to the Atlas CLI
sections.body:
In your terminal, connect to your local deployment from the
Atlas CLI. To learn more, see
[Connect from the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli).

sections.heading: Step 3: Create a  index.
sections.body:
Run the [atlas deployments search indexes create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-deployments-search-indexes-create)
command in your terminal, replacing `<path-to-file>` with the
path to the `search-index.json` file:```shell
atlas deployments search indexes create --file <path-to-file>
```The MongoDB Search `number` type has the following parameters:| UI Field Name  | JSON Option    | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Default |
| -------------- | -------------- | ------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| Data Type      | type           | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be number.                                                                                                                                                                                            |         |
| Representation | representation | string  | Optional  | Data type of the field to index. Values are:int64 - for indexing large integers without loss of precision and for rounding double values to integers. You can't use this type to index large double values.double - for indexing large double values without rounding. | double  |
| Index Integers | indexIntegers  | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing int32 and int64 type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexDoubles must be true.                                                                                                               | true    |
| Index Doubles  | indexDoubles   | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing double type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexIntegers must be true.                                                                                                                       | true    |The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.Representation ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the [sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes the
`account_id` field with 64-bit integer values. The
following example also:* Indexes all other integer values in the `account_id` field.

* Rounds any decimal values and indexes small double type
  values in the `account_id` field.```json
{
  "name": "default",
  "collectionName": "accounts",
  "database": "sample_analytics",
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": false,
    "fields": {
      "account_id": {
        "type": "number",
        "representation": "int64"
      }
    }
  }
}
```Index Integers ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes integer
values and omits doubles values in the the `account_id`.```json
{
  "name": "default",
  "collectionName": "accounts",
  "database": "sample_analytics",
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": false,
    "fields": {
      "account_id": {
        "type": "number",
        "representation": "int64",
        "indexDoubles": false
      }
    }
  }
}
```Index Doubles ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes
`double` type values and omits the 32-bit and 64-bit integer
values in the `bathrooms` field.```json
{
  "name": "default",
  "collectionName": "listingsAndReviews",
  "database": "sample_airbnb",
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": false,
    "fields": {
      "bathrooms": {
        "type": "number",
        "indexIntegers": false
      }
    }
  }
}
```You can use the MongoDB Search `number` type to index fields with numeric
values of `int32`, `int64`, and `double` data types.
You can use the [equals](equals-ref), [range](range-ref), and [near](near-ref)
operators to query indexed fields of type `number`. You can also run a
[faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) query on `number` type fields.> **Note:** To query numeric values in arrays, you can use only the [range](range-ref)
> operator.MongoDB Search automatically indexes all numeric fields in indexes created after
[July 2023](fts20230710) for [sorting](sort-ref) the MongoDB Search
results. For preexisting indexes, you must rebuild the index to use
number fields in the index for sorting. To learn more, see
[index update](fts-index-rebuild) and [sorting](sort-ref).If you enable [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings), MongoDB Search
automatically indexes fields of type dateFacet. You can
use the **Visual Editor** or the  **JSON Editor** in the Atlas UI to
index fields as the dateFacet type.

sections.heading: Step 1: Connect to your cluster using .
sections.body:
Open [Compass](https://www.mongodb.com/try/download/compass) and connect to your cluster. For
detailed instructions, see [Compass](atlas-connect-via-compass).

sections.heading: Step 2: Specify the database and collection.
sections.body: On the Database screen, click the name of the database, then click the name of the collection.

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the  index.
Step 1: Connect to the deployment by using .
sections.body:
In your terminal, connect to your Atlas cloud-hosted
deployment or local deployment from [mongosh](mongodb-shell). For detailed
instructions on how to connect, see
[Connect to a Deployment](mongodb-shell/connect).

sections.heading: Step 2: Switch to the database that contains the collection for which you want to create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**

```shell
 use <database>
```

**Output:**

```shell
switched to db <database>
```

sections.heading: Step 3: Run the  method to create the index.
Step 1: Set up the C project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` for this project:

```shell
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
```

Add the C driver to your project by following the instructions in the
[MongoDB C Driver documentation](languages/c/c-driver/current/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Set up a CMake application
sections.body:
To configure your application, create a `CMakeLists.txt` file in
your project directory. Then, add the following code to the file:

```txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

```

The preceding code performs the following actions:

- Configures a C project.

- Creates a `index.out` executable for your application.

- Finds and requires the C driver. Replace the `<version>`
  placeholder with your C driver version number, such as `2.0.0`.

- Links the program to the `libmongoc` library.

> **Note:** In the sample `CMakeLists.txt` file, the `mongoc::mongoc` target
> points to either the static library or the shared library.
> The library type depends on which one is available and
> whichever type the user specifies in the `MONGOC_DEFAULT_IMPORTED_LIBRARY_TYPE`
> CMake configuration setting. If you don't set this value and
> both library types are available, `mongoc::mongoc` uses
> the static library.
>
> You can use the `mongoc::static` target to explicitly use the
> static library or the `mongoc::shared` target to use the shared
> library.

codeBlocks.content:
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

sections.heading: Step 4: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up and initialize the .NET/C# project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` and initialize your project in that directory:

```shell
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
dotnet new console

# Add the MongoDB .NET/C# Driver to your project
dotnet add package MongoDB.Driver
```

For more detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB C# Driver documentation](csharp/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
dotnet new console

# Add the MongoDB .NET/C# Driver to your project
dotnet add package MongoDB.Driver

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.

sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` for this project:

```shell
# Create a new directory for the project

```

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up and initialize the Go module.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` and initialize your project in that directory:

```shell
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
go mod init atlas-search-project

# Add the MongoDB Go Driver to your project
go get go.mongodb.org/mongo-driver/v2/mongo
```

For more detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB Go Driver documentation](go/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
go mod init atlas-search-project

# Add the MongoDB Go Driver to your project
go get go.mongodb.org/mongo-driver/v2/mongo

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up your Java project with the MongoDB Java driver.
sections.body:
In your IDE, create a new [Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) or
[Gradle](https://gradle.org/) project. Add the Bill of
Materials (BOM) for MongoDB JVM artifacts to your project to
organize dependency versions. The BOM simplifies dependency
management by ensuring that you maintain consistent and compatible
versions of dependencies, such as between the Java driver and
the core driver library. Use the BOM to avoid version conflicts
and simplify upgrades.Select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs
to view instructions for adding the BOM for each dependency manager:MavenAdd the following code to the `dependencyManagement` list in your
`pom.xml` file:```xml
<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>
            <artifactId>mongodb-driver-bom</artifactId>
            <version>5.5.1</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>
```GradleAdd the following code to dependencies list in your
`build.gradle` file:```groovy
dependencies {
    implementation(platform("org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-bom:5.5.1"))
}
```To view a list of dependencies that the BOM manages, see
the [mongodb-driver-bom dependency listing](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/org.mongodb/mongodb-driver-bom/5.51)
on the Maven Repository website.After adding the BOM, select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs to view
instructions for adding the MongoDB Java Sync Driver as a dependency
in your project:MavenAdd the following code to the `dependencies` array in your Maven
project's `pom.xml` file:```xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>    
      <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>    
      <artifactId>mongodb-driver-sync</artifactId>    
      <version>4.11.1</version>    
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

```GradleAdd the following to the `dependencies` array in your Gradle
project's `build.gradle` file:```json
dependencies {
   // MongoDB Java Sync Driver v4.11.0 or later
   implementation 'org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync'
}

```Run your package manager to install the dependencies to your project.For more detailed installation instructions and version compatibility, see
the [MongoDB Java Driver documentation](java/sync/current/get-started).

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Compile and run the file to create the index.
Step 1: Initialize your Node.js project.
sections.body:
```shell
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
npm init -y

# Add the MongoDB Node.js Driver to your project
npm install mongodb
```

For detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB Node Driver documentation](node).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
npm init -y

# Add the MongoDB Node.js Driver to your project
npm install mongodb

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
Step 1: Set up the Python project.
sections.body:
```shell
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

# Install PyMongo
pip install pymongo
```

For detailed installation instructions, see
[MongoDB Python Driver documentation](languages/python/pymongo-driver/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

# Install PyMongo
pip install pymongo

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
Create a `create_index.py` file in your project directory,
and copy and paste the following code into the file.```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["<database>"]
collection = database["<collection>"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": True|False,
            "fields": {
                "<field_name>":{
                   "type": "number",
                   "representation": "int64|double",
                   "indexIntegers": True|False,
                   "indexDoubles": True|False
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")

```Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.Atlas ClusterYour connection string should use the following format:```
mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
```To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).Local or Self-ManagedYour connection string should use the
following format:```
mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
```To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**```shell
python create_index.py
```**Output:**```
New index name: default
```The MongoDB Search `number` type has the following parameters:| UI Field Name  | JSON Option    | Type    | Necessity | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | Default |
| -------------- | -------------- | ------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------- |
| Data Type      | type           | string  | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be number.                                                                                                                                                                                            |         |
| Representation | representation | string  | Optional  | Data type of the field to index. Values are:int64 - for indexing large integers without loss of precision and for rounding double values to integers. You can't use this type to index large double values.double - for indexing large double values without rounding. | double  |
| Index Integers | indexIntegers  | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing int32 and int64 type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexDoubles must be true.                                                                                                               | true    |
| Index Doubles  | indexDoubles   | boolean | Optional  | Flag that indicates whether to index or omit indexing double type values. Value can be true or false. Either this or indexIntegers must be true.                                                                                                                       | true    |The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.Representation ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the [sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes the
`account_id` field with 64-bit integer values. The
following example also:* Indexes all other integer values in the `account_id` field.

* Rounds any decimal values and indexes small double type
  values in the `account_id` field.```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_analytics"]
collection = database["accounts"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": False,
            "fields": {
                "account_id":{
                   "type": "number",
                   "representation": "int64"
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```Index Integers ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_analytics.accounts` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes integer
values and omits doubles values in the the `account_id`.```python

```Index Doubles ExampleThe following index definition for the
`sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets) indexes
`double` type values and omits the 32-bit and 64-bit integer
values in the `bathrooms` field.```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_airbnb"]
collection = database["listingsAndReviews"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": False,

```
